section_title: 7.10 Solar Angular Diameter: The Simplest Test (legacy id SEC-4.8 in tracker)

fingerprint_1.name: Solar orbit radius (7,400 km) is the globe polar arc to 23.44° latitude
fingerprint_1.description: Section 7.10 accepts the dome's parameter — the sun orbits the pole at a radius of approximately 7,400 km (the Tropic of Cancer in dome coordinates) — and uses it to derive the predicted angular diameter variation. This 7,400 km value is not an independent dome parameter. It is exactly the polar arc length on the globe from the geographic North Pole to latitude 23.44° (Earth's axial obliquity), computed in two equivalent ways: (a) via the dome's azimuthal-equidistant formula r = (a/2) × (1 − φ/90) with a = 20,015 km and φ = 23.44° yields 7,401.10 km; (b) via the globe geometric formula r = R_Earth × (90° − φ) × π/180 with R_Earth = 6,371 km and φ = 23.44° yields 7,401.13 km. The two agree to 0.0004% — better than the dome states the value (4 sig figs). The match is forced by construction: the dome's entire coordinate system uses a = π × R_Earth = 20,015.09 km, an azimuthal-equidistant projection of the globe that preserves polar arc lengths exactly. Once a = πR is fixed, every "dome latitude" is just the globe latitude relabeled. The Tropic of Cancer at 7,400 km is literally the globe Tropic of Cancer, projected onto the disc via a transformation that conserves the polar-radial distance.
fingerprint_1.globe_value: R_Earth × (90° − 23.44°) × π/180 = 6,371 × 1.1617 rad = 7,401.13 km (polar arc length on the globe to the Tropic of Cancer, using Earth's 23.44° obliquity)
fingerprint_1.dome_value: 7,400 km (sun's orbital radius from the pole at solstice latitude, per section 7.10)
fingerprint_1.why_it_matters: The kill shot is even stronger than the section presents. Section 7.10 already shows the dome's geometry predicts 35–63% daily angular diameter variation versus an observed <0.01%. But it accepts "7,400 km" as a free dome parameter without flagging where it came from. In fact, the dome cannot derive 7,400 km from any flat-disc principle. The Tropic of Cancer exists on the globe because Earth's rotation axis is tilted 23.44° relative to its orbit around a 150-million-km-distant sun; the Tropic is the latitude band where the sun reaches zenith at solstice. On a flat disc with a sun orbiting locally at 5,733 km altitude, there is no first-principles reason for the orbit radius to land at exactly 7,400 km rather than 5,000 or 9,000 km. The dome chose 7,400 km because the globe's 23.44° obliquity puts the Tropic there. So the dome's angular-diameter prediction is built from globe-imported geometry: π × R_Earth for the disc radius, and R_Earth × colatitude_TOC for the solar orbit. Refuting it doesn't even require the camera-and-filter test — the test is overkill, because the geometry being tested is not really a dome-physics prediction at all; it is the globe's coordinate system stretched into a disc and then asked to behave like a real cosmology. The kill shot kills twice: first by predicted-vs-observed mismatch, second by showing the prediction can only be set up by smuggling globe astronomy in through the back door.
fingerprint_1.suggested_text: A note worth adding before "the geometry" paragraph: "Note that the 7,400 km solar orbit radius the dome model uses is not derived from any flat-disc principle. It equals R_Earth × (90° − 23.44°) × π/180 = 7,401 km — the polar arc length on the globe to latitude 23.44° (Earth's axial obliquity). Combined with the disc radius a = π × R_Earth = 20,015 km, the dome's solar geometry is the globe's polar coordinate system relabeled. The 23.44° tropic exists on the globe because of the tilt of Earth's rotation axis relative to its orbit around a 150-million-km sun; on a disc with a 5,733-km local sun, there is no first-principles reason for the sun's orbital radius to land at this specific value rather than any other. The dome chose 7,400 km because that is where the globe's tropic lives. Even before we measure angular diameters, the kill shot is set up using globe-derived parameters that the dome model has no independent way to compute."

fingerprint_2.name: 23.44° obliquity is silently inherited from globe astronomy
fingerprint_2.description: Section 7.10 mentions "the Tropic of Cancer in dome coordinates" but never names the underlying parameter — Earth's 23.44° axial obliquity — that defines where the tropic is. Obliquity is a globe-cosmology quantity: the angle between Earth's rotation axis and its orbital plane around the sun. On a dome where the sun spirals between two latitudes through the year, the dome model has to pick those latitudes. It picked 23.44°N and 23.44°S — the same numbers the globe uses, with no derivation. The same number reappears in WIN-056 (already flagged in our review) where the dome's solar elevation formula uses 23.45° as a free parameter masquerading as a derivation. Section 7.10 strengthens the cluster: not just one "obliquity" appearance, but a structural parameter (the 7,400 km orbit radius) that depends on it.
fingerprint_2.dome_value: Tropic of Cancer latitude, baked into the 7,400 km solar orbit radius
fingerprint_2.why_it_matters: A dome defender could counter: "23.44° is just an empirical observation — we use what we measure." But that concedes the case. The dome claims to be a self-contained cosmology with sun and moon as physical bodies inside a finite cavity. A self-contained cosmology should derive its key angular parameters from its own geometry, not import them from a model whose validity it denies. Importing 23.44° from globe heliocentric astronomy is structurally identical to importing the orbital eccentricity, the synodic month, or any other globe-frame parameter — each one is an admission that the globe model is doing the explanatory work and the dome is overlaying a redrawn coordinate system on top.
fingerprint_2.suggested_text: Optional one-sentence add to the noon-distance paragraph: "The 23.44° latitude that defines this Tropic — and therefore the 7,400 km radius on which the dome's prediction depends — is Earth's axial obliquity. There is no flat-disc derivation of this angle; it is taken from the globe model whose validity the dome rejects."

objection_1.claim: 7,400 km could be a coincidence or an empirical fit, not a globe import.
objection_1.response: Two-method agreement to 0.0004% with no fit parameters rules this out. The disc radius a = π × R_Earth is exact to four significant figures; the solar orbit radius equals the globe polar arc length using that disc radius and the globe obliquity. There are no free parameters being tuned — the entire geometry is the globe's polar coordinate system rendered as a disc.

objection_2.claim: "Tropic of Cancer" is just a label; the dome could pick any orbital radius.
objection_2.response: It could, but then the dome would have to explain why the sun's orbital radius corresponds exactly to where ground observers experience zenith sun at solstice — the operational definition of the tropic. Both the radius value and the observational definition are inherited from globe astronomy; the dome just relabels them.

objection_3.claim: The kill shot does not actually depend on the 7,400 km value being globe-derived; the falsification works regardless.
objection_3.response: True — and that is why this fingerprint is an addition rather than a replacement. The current section's arithmetic is correct without it. The fingerprint adds depth: even before measurement, the dome had to import globe parameters to set the test up. This makes the section harder to dismiss as "naïve straight-line optics" — the geometry being tested is the dome's own geometry, but that geometry only exists because of borrowed globe astronomy.

code_search.notes: Did not run a fresh GitHub API search this run; relied on the curmudgeon's SEC-4.8 review which inspected the same source files and found no published derivation of the 7,400 km value. The dome's model page declares disc_radius = 20,015 km and sun altitude = 5,733 km but provides no derivation of the solar orbit radius. A future cycle could fetch inject_ai_layer.py to confirm the value appears as a literal constant rather than a computation; based on the WIN-056 prior (where 23.45° is hardcoded) this is the expected pattern.
